coredata.dc:title: First fluorometric sensor for dronedarone detection based on aggregation-induced quenching of red-emissive carbon dots: Application to pharmacokinetics
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Goda, Mohamed N.
Alqarni, Laila S.
Ibrahim, Hossieny
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
Ali, Al-Montaser Bellah H.
coredata.dc:description: Dronedarone (DND) is a widely used antiarrhythmic drug, and accurate determination of its concentration is crucial for therapeutic monitoring and pharmacokinetic studies. In this work, we report the first fluorometric method for the detection of dronedarone, based on the quenching effect of DND on the red emission of carbon dots (RCDs). The detection mechanism relies on electrostatic interactions between the positively charged DND molecules and negatively charged RCDs in an acidic medium, which induces aggregation of the RCDs and results in significant fluorescence quenching. This quenching enables highly sensitive quantification of DND, achieving a detection limit of 2.31 ng/mL and a wide linear range of 5–200 ng/mL with excellent linearity (R2 = 0.9985). The mechanism of detection and thorough characterization of the RCDs were comprehensively investigated. The proposed method also demonstrated outstanding selectivity when applied to both spiked and real rat plasma samples, supporting its potential use in pharmacokinetic evaluations of dronedarone.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dronedarone
Fluorescence quenching
Carbon dots
Fluorometric method
Electrostatic interaction

Structural and morphological studies of RCDs
Selectivity of RCDs toward DND detection
Application of method to pharmacokinetic study

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported and funded by the Deanship of Scientific Research at Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU) (grant number IMSIU-DDRSP2501).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of RCDs characterization: (A) TEM image and particle size histogram (inset); (B) XRD pattern; (C) FTIR spectra showing functional groups; and (D) elemental analysis by XPS.

(A) Optical properties of RCDs illustrated through UV–vis absorption, fluorescence, and excitation spectra (Inset: RCDs visible emission under UV light). (B) Photoluminescence emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths from 560 to 630 nm.
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of RCDs measured at increasing DND concentrations (0–200.0 ng/mL); (B) Linear dependence of ΔF on DND concentration within the range of 5.0–200.0 ng/mL.
Response of the RCDs to different (A) biological molecules and other co-administered drugs, and (B) metal ions and anions.
(A) Zeta potential of RCDs before and after addition of DND. TEM images of (B) RCDs, (C) RCDs + DND. (D) relationship between ΔF and NaCl concentration (0.1–1.0 M).
Recovery of DND in plasma samples (n = 3).
Pharmacokinetic parameters of DND in healthy rats (n = 8).

head.ce:title: First fluorometric sensor for dronedarone detection based on aggregation-induced quenching of red-emissive carbon dots: Application to pharmacokinetics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU), Riyadh 11623, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Assiut University, Assiut 71516, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dronedarone (DND) is a widely used antiarrhythmic drug, and accurate determination of its concentration is crucial for therapeutic monitoring and pharmacokinetic studies. In this work, we report the first fluorometric method for the detection of dronedarone, based on the quenching effect of DND on the red emission of carbon dots (RCDs). The detection mechanism relies on electrostatic interactions between the positively charged DND molecules and negatively charged RCDs in an acidic medium, which induces aggregation of the RCDs and results in significant fluorescence quenching. This quenching enables highly sensitive quantification of DND, achieving a detection limit of 2.31 ng/mL and a wide linear range of 5–200 ng/mL with excellent linearity (R2 = 0.9985). The mechanism of detection and thorough characterization of the RCDs were comprehensively investigated. The proposed method also demonstrated outstanding selectivity when applied to both spiked and real rat plasma samples, supporting its potential use in pharmacokinetic evaluations of dronedarone.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dronedarone (DND), an antiarrhythmic medication primarily used for the treatment of cardiac arrhythmia such as atrial fibrillation, works by modulating multiple ion channels (sodium, potassium, and calcium) to restore normal heart rhythm while also possessing anti-adrenergic properties to reduce heart rate [1]. Typically administered at a maintenance dose of 400 mg twice daily with meals, therapeutic plasma concentrations range between 84 and 167 ng/mL [2], though significant inter-individual variability exists in its pharmacokinetics [3]. Common side effects include gastrointestinal disturbances, fatigue, and bradycardia, while elevated plasma levels may lead to more serious complications including QT prolongation, potentially life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias, and severe hepatotoxicity—notably, DND is contraindicated in patients with severe heart failure or permanent atrial fibrillation due to increased mortality risk [4]. DND offers several advantages over amiodarone, primarily in terms of safety and tolerability. Unlike amiodarone, which contains iodine and is associated with significant long-term toxicities affecting the thyroid, lungs, liver, and skin, DND is a non-iodinated analog designed to reduce these organ-specific side effects. It has a shorter half-life, leading to quicker clearance from the body, and fewer drug interactions. While both drugs are used to manage atrial fibrillation, DND is generally considered a safer alternative for patients who cannot tolerate the adverse effects of amiodarone, especially for long-term rhythm control [5]. Accurate determination of DND plasma concentrations is crucial for optimizing therapeutic efficacy, minimizing toxicity, ensuring patient compliance, and guiding dosage adjustments in special populations such as elderly patients or those with hepatic impairment, ultimately improving treatment outcomes and patient safety.

Previous methods for determining DND in biological samples have relied primarily on liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [6,7], which offers high sensitivity and specificity but requires expensive instrumentation, specialized training, and complex sample preparation. High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with UV detection has been employed as a more accessible alternative [8], though with reduced sensitivity compared to LC-MS/MS methods. These conventional techniques, while effective, typically suffer from lengthy analysis times, labor-intensive extraction procedures, and considerable solvent consumption, limiting their utility for routine clinical monitoring. Spectrophotometric determination of DND is limited by low selectivity in complex matrices due to overlapping absorbance with other substances [9]. Some approaches have attempted to overcome these limitations through solid-phase extraction or protein precipitation techniques [10], but these modifications often add complexity and cost without fully resolving the underlying issues of throughput and accessibility needed for widespread therapeutic drug monitoring of DND in clinical practice.

Fluorescence detection methods offer significant advantages for pharmaceutical analysis, including exceptional sensitivity, superior selectivity, minimal background interference, and wide dynamic range—often detecting compounds at picomolar concentrations [11]. These methods typically require simple instrumentation, provide rapid analysis times, and enable simultaneous detection of multiple analytes, making them particularly valuable for complex biological matrices [12–16]. Carbon dots have emerged as revolutionary fluorescent nanomaterials that further enhance these capabilities through their unique properties: exceptional photostability compared to traditional organic fluorophores, tunable emission wavelengths, remarkable quantum yields, and resistance to photobleaching [17]. Additionally, carbon dots possess excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and can be synthesized from inexpensive, environmentally friendly precursors through straightforward methods. Their surface can be readily functionalized to enhance selectivity toward specific analytes, creating “turn-on” or “turn-off” fluorescent sensors with high specificity [18]. The combination of these attributes makes carbon dot-based fluorescence detection an increasingly attractive approach for sensitive and selective determination of pharmaceuticals, including challenging compounds like DND in complex biological samples [19].

This work represents the first fluorometric method for DND determination utilizing red-emitting carbon dots (RCDs). The detection mechanism exploits the electrostatic attraction between the positively charged DND molecules and negatively charged RCDs at acidic medium, leading to carbon dot aggregation and subsequent fluorescence quenching that enables sensitive quantification. In contrast to these conventional approaches, the red-emitting carbon-dot assay offers significant analytical and practical advantages. The method eliminates the need for organic solvents, complex extraction procedures, and specialized instrumentation, operating instead through a simple “mix-and-measure” protocol that requires only a handheld fluorimeter. This streamlined approach achieves nM-level sensitivity within three minutes using a single-buffer system, dramatically reducing both analysis time and operational complexity. The carbon dot platform demonstrates superior matrix tolerance, avoiding the surface-fouling and drift issues that plague electrochemical sensors, while maintaining excellent selectivity without the carry-over effects common in chromatographic methods. Most importantly, the assay's minimal equipment requirements and user-friendly protocol make it ideally suited for point-of-care applications, enabling cost-effective DND monitoring at the bedside or in outpatient settings where conventional methods are impractical to deploy.

DND (99.0 % purity) was acquired from Eva Pharma in Cairo, Egypt, while Dronadex® tablets (400 mg DND) were obtained from a local pharmacy. Various compounds including uric acid, glutathione, glucose, ascorbic acid, lactose, and several pharmaceuticals (simvastatin, warfarin, metoprolol, verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin, sotalol, and propranolol) were sourced from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). Copper sulfate, calcium chloride, magnesium chloride, iron(III) chloride, boric acid, iron(II) chloride, phosphoric acid, sodium phosphate, sodium sulfate, acetic acid, and sodium chloride were purchased from El-Nasr Pharmaceutical Chemicals Company in Cairo, Egypt. Throughout all experimental procedures, ultra-pure double-distilled water (18.2 MΩ·cm) was utilized.
Comprehensive instrumental specifications and parameters are detailed in the Supplementary Information.

RCDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal approach. The process involved dissolving terephthalic acid (0.07 g) and sulphanilic acid (0.2 g) in 20 mL of water, followed by heating the solution at 200 °C for 12 h in a Teflon-lined reactor. Upon cooling to room temperature, the mixture underwent centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. The resulting supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and subsequently dialyzed using a 1000 Da cutoff membrane for 24 h. The purified solution was then lyophilized to yield RCDs in powder form. The stable RCDs powder was stored at 4 °C until needed for further experimental use. Hydrothermal synthesis using 0.27 g of precursors (terephthalic acid and sulphanilic acid in a 1:3 ratio) yielded 24.8 ± 1.2 mg of carbon dots, corresponding to a 9.2 ± 0.4 % yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield of the prepared RCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Quantum yield calculations are provided in the Supplementary Information.

The detection system was established by combining 500 µL of RCDs solution (1.0 mg/mL) with 100 µL of varying concentrations of DND in a 10.0 mL volumetric flask. The final volume was adjusted using Britton-Robinson buffer at pH 4.0. The reaction mixture was allowed to stand for 2.0 min before measurement. Fluorescence measurements were carried out with an excitation wavelength of 600 nm and an emission wavelength of 680 nm. The excitation and emission monochromators were configured with a 10 nm bandpass width for all readings.

The sample preparation procedures was adapted from a previously reported protocol [20]. Plasma samples (1000 μL) were placed in capped 1.5 mL Eppendorf tubes and fortified with different concentrations of 100 μL of DND standard solution. For consistent deproteinization across all samples, 100 μL of methanol was introduced followed by brief vortexing (20 s). The samples were then acidified with 25 μL of glacial acetic acid, vortexed thoroughly for 30 s, and centrifuged at 10,000g for 10 min to separate precipitated proteins. Clean-up was performed via solid phase extraction using Isolute® SCX cartridges mounted on a vacuum manifold system. Prior to sample application, each cartridge was prepared by sequential conditioning with 1 mL methanol followed by 1 mL water. The acidified supernatant was carefully loaded onto the preconditioned cartridge and allowed to flow through at a controlled rate. Washing steps included 1 mL of 0.1 M hydrochloric acid followed by 1 mL of methanol to remove matrix interferences. The target analytes were subsequently recovered using 2 mL of a ternary elution solvent (methylene chloride/isopropanol/ammonia, 78/20/2, v/v/v). The collected eluate was evaporated under a gentle stream of nitrogen at 40 °C, and the resulting residue was reconstituted in ethanol for analysis.

Male Sprague-Dawley rats (n = 6, weighing 200 ± 20 g) were acquired from Assiut University's Animal Center in Egypt. The animals were maintained with unrestricted access to food and water for a minimum adaptation period of seven days in an approved laboratory environment. All experimental procedures complied with Assiut University's ethical guidelines and the principles outlined in the Declaration of Helsinki.

Following an overnight fast of at least 12 h, each rat received a single oral dose of DND (35 mg/kg) suspended in 0.5 % sodium carboxymethyl cellulose solution. Blood samples (approximately 0.5 mL) were collected via tail vein at multiple timepoints: pre-dose (0 h) and 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24, 36, and 48 h post-administration. The samples were collected in 1.5 mL heparinized polyethylene tubes and subsequently centrifuged at 8000g for 10 min at 4 °C. The resulting plasma was separated and stored at −80 °C until analysis. Plasma concentrations were determined using a fluorescent probe technique, and pharmacokinetic parameters were subsequently calculated using non-compartmental analysis with pK solver software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological studies of RCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis of the synthesized red-emitting carbon dots revealed predominantly near-spherical nanoparticles with well-defined boundaries and relatively uniform morphology. The RCDs exhibited a narrow particle size distribution ranging from 2.2 nm to 6.4 nm in diameter (average diameter is 3.9 nm), confirming their successful synthesis within the quantum confinement regime (<10 nm) necessary for their distinctive optical properties (Fig. 1 A). The absence of significant aggregation in the TEM image suggests good colloidal stability, likely attributable to the negative surface charge from incorporated sulfonate groups that provide electrostatic repulsion between individual RCDs in solution. Complementary high-resolution TEM images display clear lattice fringes with an average interplanar spacing of ≈0.21 nm, which can be ascribed to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon, confirming that small crystalline graphitic domains are embedded within the otherwise amorphous carbon matrix. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the synthesized red-emitting carbon dots exhibited a characteristic broad peak centered at 2θ = 20.33° (Fig. 1B), which is consistent with the typical amorphous carbon structure commonly observed in RCDs [21]. The FTIR spectrum of the red-emitting carbon dots reveals a characteristic fingerprint pattern confirming their successful synthesis from terephthalic acid and sulphanilic acid precursors (Fig. 1C). The broad absorption band at 3573 cm−1 corresponds to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of hydroxyl and amine functionalities on the carbon dot surface [22]. The peaks at 1971 cm−1 and 1802 cm−1 can be attributed to C=O stretching vibrations from carboxylic acid and carbonyl groups. The band at 1383 cm−1 represents C–N stretching or potentially sulfonate group vibrations, while the signal at 1288 cm−1 indicates C–O stretching in carboxylic acids or phenolic compounds [23]. The distinct peak at 1149 cm−1 corresponds to S=O stretching vibrations from sulfonate groups derived from sulphanilic acid, confirming successful incorporation of sulfur-containing moieties. The bands at 850 cm−1 and 661 cm−1 can be assigned to aromatic C–H out-of-plane bending vibrations and S-O stretching, respectively, while the peak at 488 cm−1 likely represents metal–oxygen or other skeletal vibrations in the carbon dot framework [24]. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of red-emitting carbon dots revealed a comprehensive elemental composition profile (Fig. 1D). The full spectrum exhibited characteristic peaks at 283.7 eV, 395.9 eV, 531.1 eV, and 161.1 eV, corresponding to the presence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur, respectively, confirming the successful incorporation of all precursor elements into the carbon dot structure. Detailed high-resolution spectral analysis of the carbon region showed three distinct peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 288.8 eV, which can be attributed to C–C/C=C (sp2 hybridized carbon), C-O/C-N (carbon–oxygen/carbon–nitrogen single bonds), and C=O/COOH (carbonyl/carboxylic groups) functionalities, respectively [25] (Fig. S1A). The nitrogen spectrum revealed peaks at 398.4 eV, 399.1 eV, and 400.6 eV, corresponding to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, and graphitic-N species, indicative of nitrogen doping within the carbon dot framework (Fig. S1B). Oxygen-containing functionalities were evidenced by peaks at 531.7 eV and 535.9 eV, representing C=O (carbonyl) and C–O (hydroxyl/epoxy) groups (Fig. S1C), while the sulfur region displayed signals at 169.6 eV and 170.4 eV, characteristic of oxidized sulfur species (sulfonate/sulfate groups) derived from the sulphanilic acid precursor [26] (Fig. S1D), which contribute to the surface functionality and negative charge that enables interaction with DND.

The optical characterization of the synthesized red-emitting carbon dots revealed a complex absorption and emission profile consistent with their unique electronic structure. The absorption spectrum displayed two prominent peaks at 248 nm and 287 nm, attributable to π–π* transitions of aromatic sp2 carbon domains and n–π* transitions of surface functional groups containing C=O, C=N, and C-S bonds, respectively [27] (Fig. 2 A). The presence of a broad, low-intensity absorption band in the 500–600 nm region indicates successful introduction of long-wavelength absorbing chromophores, likely resulting from extended π-conjugation and surface states [28]. Fluorescence measurements confirmed the desired red emission characteristics, with excitation at 600 nm yielding a maximum emission at 680 nm—placing these RCDs firmly in the red region of the electromagnetic spectrum. Notably, when various excitation wavelengths (560–630 nm) were tested (Fig. 2B), the emission wavelength remained constant at 680 nm, demonstrating excitation-independent emission behavior that suggests a homogeneous fluorophore population with well-defined energy levels [29]. The measured quantum yield of 23.25 % is remarkably high for red-emitting carbon dots, indicating efficient radiative relaxation pathways and making these nanomaterials exceptionally suitable for sensitive analytical applications, including the fluorometric determination of DND.

A thorough investigation of the synthesized RCDs stability was conducted across multiple environmental parameters by tracking fluorescence intensity as the primary indicator of system integrity. The pH-dependent analysis revealed optimal and consistent fluorescence performance up to pH 8, after which a notable decline in signal intensity was observed, suggesting potential protonation/deprotonation effects on surface functional groups that influence emission properties [30] (Fig. S2A). The RCDs exhibited exceptional tolerance to varying ionic strengths when tested with NaCl solutions ranging from 0.01 to 2.0 M (Fig. S2B), maintaining stable fluorescence output throughout—a critical advantage for analytical applications in complex biological samples containing various electrolytes. When subjected to prolonged UV radiation for periods extending to 5 h (Fig. S2C), the RCDs displayed impressive resistance to photobleaching, experiencing only minimal reduction in fluorescence intensity, which underscores their suitability for extended analytical procedures requiring consistent optical performance. Temperature stability assessment between 20 °C and 100 °C demonstrated that the RCDs retain their fluorescence characteristics reliably up to 60 °C (Fig. S2D), with noticeable alterations emerging at higher temperatures, likely due to structural changes or aggregation phenomena [31]. This multifaceted stability profile confirms the RCDs' durability across diverse experimental conditions, establishing their potential as dependable fluorescent probes for sensing applications.

Storage stability was evaluated by monitoring the fluorescence intensity of the RCDs over 15 days (Fig. S3). Samples kept in the dark at room temperature were measured on days 0, 3, 6, 9, 12, and 15; the normalized emission intensity remained within ±2 % of the initial value, with no peak shift or broadening. This negligible change confirms that the dots maintain excellent photostability and resist aggregation for at least two weeks under ambient conditions.

Optimization of the detection conditions for DND was systematically performed by monitoring changes in fluorescence intensity (ΔF) at 680 nm. pH investigation revealed a critical influence on the sensing mechanism, with signal response increasing markedly until reaching a maximum at pH 4.0, followed by subsequent decline at higher pH values (Fig. S4A). This optimal pH creates ideal conditions for the electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged RCDs (due to ionized sulfonate and carboxylate groups) and the positively charged DND molecules (protonated at the tertiary amino group). At pH 4.0, the degree of ionization achieves the perfect balance—sufficient negative charge density on the carbon dot surface to promote effective electrostatic attraction with DND, yet not excessive as to cause repulsion or competitive binding with buffer components at higher pH values. Kinetic analysis demonstrated rapid interaction dynamics, with the fluorescence response reaching equilibrium within just 2.0 min and remaining stable thereafter, indicating fast aggregation of RCDs induced by DND binding (Fig. S4B). This short reaction time, combined with the optimized pH conditions, enables efficient analytical procedures with minimal waiting periods, highlighting the practical utility of this fluorescence quenching method for rapid DND determination in various sample matrices.

Under optimized conditions, the RCDs sensing platform for DND demonstrated exceptional analytical performance. The introduction of increasing DND concentrations to the RCDs solution resulted in systematic fluorescence quenching at 680 nm (Fig. 3 A). Quantification was achieved by calculating the fluorescence intensity difference (ΔF = F0 − F), where F0 represents the initial RCDs fluorescence without analyte and F indicates the fluorescence after DND addition. The analytical response curve exhibited remarkable linearity across a concentration range of 5.0–200.0 ng/mL, with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9985 confirming the robust linear relationship (Fig. 3B). Sensitivity assessment was performed by determining the limit of detection (LOD) through the standard method using the formula 3σ/K (where σ represents the standard deviation of blank measurements and K is the calibration curve slope). The calculated LOD value of 2.31 ng/mL highlights the impressive sensitivity of this fluorescence-based detection system, making it suitable for detecting therapeutically relevant concentrations of DND in clinical samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of RCDs toward DND detection
ce:section.ce:para: A comprehensive interference analysis was conducted to assess the selectivity of the developed RCDs for DND determination. The study examined potential interfering substances including endogenous compounds (uric acid, glutathione, glucose, ascorbic acid, lactose), common co-administered medications (simvastatin, warfarin, metoprolol, verapamil, diltiazem, digoxin, sotalol, and propranolol), and various ions (Cu2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, PO4 3−, SO4 2−, Cl−, and Na+). When DND was present at 50.0 ng/mL and all potential interferents were introduced at significantly higher concentrations (500.0 ng/mL), no appreciable alteration in the fluorescence response (ΔF) was observed (Fig. 4 ). This exceptional selectivity, maintained even with interfering substances at tenfold higher concentrations than the target analyte, validates the highly specific nature of the interaction between the RCDs and DND. Such remarkable discrimination capability ensures reliable detection in complex biological matrices where multiple potential interferents may coexist with the target drug.

The detection mechanism for DND using RCDs leverages fundamental electrostatic principles to achieve high sensitivity and selectivity. At the optimized acidic conditions (pH 4.0), DND becomes positively charged due to protonation of its tertiary nitrogen moiety, while the RCDs maintain negative surface charge from ionized sulfonate and carboxylate groups derived from sulphanilic acid and terephthalic acid precursors. This charge complementarity facilitates strong electrostatic attraction between DND molecules and RCDs, triggering aggregation of the nanoparticles. The aggregation process disrupts the electronic structure and energy transfer pathways within the RCDs, resulting in effective fluorescence quenching observed at 680 nm. This electrostatic interaction-based mechanism offers superior sensitivity compared to alternative approaches relying on spectral overlap between carbon dot excitation and DND absorption profiles as demonstrated from preliminary trials. Furthermore, the utilization of red-emitting carbon dots provides a significant analytical advantage over conventional blue-emitting carbon dots, as the longer emission wavelength (680 nm) minimizes potential interference from endogenous fluorescent biomolecules and pharmaceutical compounds that typically emit in the blue region, thereby enhancing method selectivity when analyzing complex biological matrices.

The interaction mechanism between DND and the red-emitting carbon dots was confirmed through zeta potential analysis (Fig. 5 A), which measured a substantial negative charge (−12.6 mV) on the RCDs surface, establishing their anionic character. This negative charge arises from the ionized sulfonate and carboxylate groups derived from the precursors. In acidic conditions, DND (with a pKa of approximately 9.4 for its tertiary amine group) exists predominantly in its protonated form, carrying a positive charge. As DND molecules interact with the negatively charged RCDs, the zeta potential shifts toward more positive value (1.5 mV), indicating neutralization of the surface charge as the positively charged drug binds to the carbon dot surface. This electrostatic attraction induces aggregation of the RCDs, disrupting their electronic properties and resulting in observable fluorescence quenching. The pH-dependency studies further validated this mechanism—at lower pH values where DND is fully protonated, stronger interactions occur leading to more pronounced aggregation and quenching. At higher pH values approaching DND's pKa [32], the drug molecules become increasingly deprotonated and neutral, causing the zeta potential to stabilize as electrostatic interactions weaken, thereby decreasing the quenching effect through reduced aggregation of the RCDs (Fig. S3A).

Additional verification of the proposed mechanism was provided by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) examination, which captured the morphological changes of RCDs following exposure to DND. The comparative TEM images revealed significant alterations in particle distribution and size characteristics upon drug addition (Fig. 5B and C). While RCDs appeared as well-dispersed, discrete nanoparticles, the DND-treated sample displayed pronounced clustering and formation of larger particulate assemblies. This observed aggregation phenomenon provides compelling visual evidence that supports the hypothesized detection mechanism—the positively charged DND molecules act as electrostatic bridges between negatively charged RCDs, inducing their proximity and subsequent aggregation. These structural changes disrupt the electronic confinement and surface states responsible for the fluorescence properties, directly correlating with the observed quenching of emission intensity at 680 nm. The TEM results establish a clear structure–function relationship that explains the analytical response of the system to DND presence.

To further substantiate the electrostatic interaction mechanism, we examined the influence of ionic strength on fluorescence quenching by adding NaCl at varying concentrations (0.1–1.0 M) to the reaction mixture (Fig. 5D). The addition of NaCl resulted in a progressive decrease in the fluorescence quenching effect, with higher salt concentrations causing greater reduction in the analytical signal. This behavior can be attributed to the screening effect of the sodium and chloride ions, which shield the electrostatic interactions between the negatively charged RCDs and positively charged DND molecules. As ionic strength increases, the electrical double layer surrounding the RCDs becomes compressed [33], reducing the effective range of electrostatic attraction and preventing efficient aggregation. This salt-dependent response provides compelling evidence for the proposed electrostatic mechanism, as non-electrostatic interactions would be minimally affected by changes in ionic strength.

The electronic transition mechanism underlying the fluorescence quenching involves fundamental alterations in the photophysical properties of RCDs upon DND interaction. Under normal conditions, the red emission at 680 nm predominantly originates from surface state-mediated transitions involving localized electronic states associated with sulfonate and carboxylate functional groups, which act as emission centers through trap-state mechanisms [34]. Unlike smaller carbon dots where quantum confinement effects dominate, red-emitting RCDs (typically > 3 nm) rely primarily on these surface/defect states for their photoluminescence, as the band-edge emission contribution becomes negligible at larger sizes [35].

Upon DND binding and RCD aggregation, the electronic structure is disrupted through three mechanistically distinct pathways. First, charge neutralization at the RCD surface directly alters the electron density distribution around chromophoric functional groups, reducing radiative transition probability from these critical surface states. Second, the close proximity of aggregated RCDs (<10 nm) enables exciton–exciton annihilation processes at the excitation fluence employed (where surface particle densities reach ∼1013 cm−2), where multiple excited states interact destructively, converting excitation energy into heat rather than photons [36]. Additionally, through-space charge transfer interactions between adjacent particles create new non-radiative decay pathways that compete with the native emission [37]. Third, aggregation generates interfacial charge-transfer states and excimer-like complexes that exhibit substantially quenched emission compared to isolated RCDs [38].

The static nature of the quenching mechanism is confirmed through multiple complementary measurements. Fluorescence lifetime measurements (τ0 = 4.2 ns for RCDs alone vs. τ = 4.1 ns in presence of DND) show no significant change within experimental error, ruling out dynamic collisional quenching processes (Fig. S5A). Stern-Volmer analysis following the equation: F0/F = 1 + Ksv[Q], where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of quencher (DND), respectively, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and [Q] is the concentration of the quencher [39]. Critically, the Stern-Volmer slopes decreased systematically with increasing temperature (298 K > 313 K > 333 K), which is diagnostic of static quenching since dynamic quenching constants typically increase with temperature due to enhanced molecular motion and collision frequency (Fig. S5B). Inner-filter effects and spectral overlap between DND absorption and RCD emission were ruled out based on the optical densities and spectral positions involved. This static quenching behavior confirms that the DND-RCD interaction occurs through stable electrostatic binding that pre-organizes the system into non-emissive or weakly emissive aggregated states before photoexcitation, consistent with the proposed aggregation-induced quenching mechanism.

To establish the practical applicability of the developed RCDs fluorescent sensor, comprehensive validation studies were performed using plasma as a complex biological matrix. Quality control experiments involved fortifying plasma samples with predetermined concentrations of DND (5.0, 20.0, and 30.0 ng/mL) followed by analysis using the optimized method. The accuracy assessment through recovery analysis yielded exceptional results, with recovery rates between 97.60 % and 98.80 % across the entire concentration range examined (Table 1 ). These near-quantitative recovery values indicate minimal matrix interference from plasma constituents, despite its complex composition containing proteins, electrolytes, and various endogenous compounds. The consistently high recovery percentages, accompanied by low relative standard deviations, demonstrate the method's remarkable resistance to biological matrix effects—a critical advantage over conventional analytical techniques that often suffer from significant signal suppression or enhancement in plasma samples. To verify the method's accuracy, we conducted comparative analyses between our fluorescence technique and reported UPLC-MS/MS for determining DND recovery in plasma samples [7]. The recovery percentages obtained showed excellent agreement between both analytical approaches, statistically confirming the method's reliability. These findings validate that our RCD-based fluorescence detection system achieves comparable precision to established chromatographic methods for DND quantification in biological matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of method to pharmacokinetic study
ce:section.ce:para: The developed fluorescence-based analytical method was successfully employed to study the pharmacokinetic behavior of DND in rats following oral administration. Blood samples were collected at predetermined time intervals, and the obtained plasma drug concentrations were analyzed using the validated protocol. As illustrated in Fig. S6, all measured plasma drug levels remained within the method's established linear dynamic range, demonstrating its capability to accurately determine both peak (Cmax) and trough (Cmin) concentrations throughout the study period. The calculated pharmacokinetic parameters, presented in Table 2 , offered valuable quantitative data regarding the drug's absorption, distribution, and clearance patterns in the experimental animals. The pharmacokinetic data indicate that dronedarone exhibits a relatively long elimination half-life (t1/2 = 49.44 h) and a prolonged mean residence time (MRT = 60.72 h), suggesting sustained systemic exposure. The drug reaches its peak plasma concentration (Cmax = 27 ng/mL) at Tmax = 3h, indicating moderate absorption. The area under the curve values (AUC0–t = 478.5 ng.h/mL, AUC0–∞ = 835.14 ng.h/mL) reflect substantial drug exposure, while the apparent volume of distribution (Vz/F = 2.99 mg/(ng/mL)) suggests extensive tissue distribution. The relatively low clearance rate (Cl/F = 0.042 mg/(ng/mL)/h) further supports slow elimination, consistent with the observed long half-life. Overall, these parameters point to a drug with prolonged activity and slow systemic clearance.

In conclusion, this groundbreaking study introduces a novel fluorometric methodology for dronedarone detection that significantly advances pharmaceutical analytical techniques. By ingeniously exploiting the electrostatic interactions between positively charged dronedarone molecules and negatively charged red emission carbon dots, we have developed a highly sensitive and selective detection approach that overcomes traditional analytical limitations. The method's remarkable performance—characterized by an exceptional limit of detection, a broad linear detection range, and impressive correlation coefficient—demonstrates its potential for precise pharmacokinetic investigations. Moreover, the successful validation using spiked and authentic rat plasma samples underscores the method's robust applicability in real-world pharmaceutical analysis. Beyond its immediate utility in dronedarone quantification, this research provides a conceptual framework for developing advanced fluorometric detection strategies, potentially inspiring future innovations in drug analysis, biomedical research, and clinical diagnostics by offering a more refined, sensitive, and versatile analytical approach.

Mohamed N. Goda: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Laila S. Alqarni: Software, Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization. Hossieny Ibrahim: Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Resources. Al-Montaser Bellah H. Ali: Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.
This work was supported and funded by the Deanship of Scientific Research at Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU) (grant number IMSIU-DDRSP2501).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported and funded by the Deanship of Scientific Research at Imam Mohammad Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU) (grant number IMSIU-DDRSP2501).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2025.116535.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
